data.title: Here's why Viasat slipped after 132% YTD rally: higher targets post Q4 were not enough
data.description:
Viasat (VSAT) stock price dropped over ~8% on Friday to ~$79.44, as investors locked in profits after the stock delivered a massive ~132.59% YTD gain, sharply outperforming the broader S&P 500 (SP500) return of 10.49%.

The pullback came after the satellite communications company posted improved Q4 FY2026 results and received fresh bullish calls from Wall Street analysts.

Viasat reported Q4 revenue of $1.17B, up 2% YoY, while net income came in at $58.8M as compared to a loss of $246.1M last year. The turnaround was largely supported by a $168.1M gain from the sale of its Navarino investment.

Moreover, its adjusted EBITDA slipped slightly to $369.9M from $374.8M, while new contract awards rose 9% YoY to $1.28B.

For the full year, revenue reached a record $4.64B, up 3% YoY, while adjusted EBITDA held steady at a record $1.55B. Importantly, the company generated positive free cash flow for the fifth straight quarter.

The company also reduced total debt to $6.59B and ended the year with $2.9B in liquidity.

While headline numbers looked stronger, investors appeared cautious around slower near-term profitability growth and flat FY2027 EBITDA guidance. 

The company expects FY2027 adjusted EBITDA to remain flat to slightly higher YoY, while free cash flow guidance of nearly $180M, excluding non-recurring Ligado payments, also disappointed some traders after the stock’s sharp run-up this year.

Still, it showed strength in other areas. Defense and Advanced Technologies revenue climbed 12% YoY to $361M, while full-year contract awards hit a record $4.93B, with backlog rising 15% to $4.07B.

Wall Street stayed optimistic after earnings. B. Riley reportedly raised its price target on Viasat to $106 from $94 with a Buy rating, citing growing momentum in defense, spectrum, and satellite services. 

Meanwhile, according to TheFly, Raymond James increased its target to $93 from $74 and maintained an Outperform rating, highlighting upside from the company’s Defense and Advanced Technologies business and potential strategic spin-off plans.

data.title: Asana expects FY 2027 revenue of $855.5M-$863.5M as it adds StackAI and guides Q2 revenue to $213M-$215M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Asana (ASAN) Q1 FY 2027

Management View

  • “We delivered a strong start to the year with revenue of $205.1 million, up 9.5% year-over-year and above the high end of our guidance,” said CEO Daniel Rogers, adding, “we’re seeing positive trends across customer retention, expansion and AI product adoption.”

  • Rogers highlighted retention and vertical momentum, saying “overall in-quarter NRR improved for the fourth consecutive quarter to 97%,” and added, “in the technology sector, we returned to positive year-over-year growth for the first time in 8 quarters.”

  • On AI monetization and product traction, Rogers said, “Taken together, our AI product bookings now represent 17% of net new ARR in Q1,” and “the number of customers spending over $100,000 annually on AI Studio nearly doubled.”

  • Rogers announced M&A, stating, “today, we’re announcing the acquisition of StackAI,” and described the rationale as, “StackAI is the logical evolution of AI Studio and accelerates our road map by over a year.”

  • “Q1 revenues were at $205.1 million, up 9.5% year-over-year,” CFO Aziz Megji said, while also noting, “We delivered an 11.5% non-GAAP operating margin or $23.6 million of operating income.”

Outlook

  • Megji guided Q2 revenue to “$213 million to $215 million,” and said the range “includes an expected contribution from StackAI of approximately 50 basis points to growth.”

  • Megji guided Q2 profitability to “non-GAAP operating income…$18 million to $20 million,” and “non-GAAP net income per share…$0.08 to $0.09.”

  • For FY 2027, Megji forecast revenue of “$855.5 million to $863.5 million,” with “non-GAAP operating margin of at least 9.75%,” and “non-GAAP net income of $0.37 per share.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s framing, Megji said, “the core assumptions underlying the FY ’27 outlook we shared last quarter remain largely unchanged,” including that “PLG remains a near-term headwind,” and guidance “does not factor in the improvement we have seen in the tech vertical over the past two quarters.”

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided in the prompt, but the fiscal-quarter format does not match the required Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 validation, so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • Megji reported customer scale and mix, stating, “We have 26,103 core customers,” and “this cohort represented 76% of our revenues in Q1,” alongside “817 customers spending $100,000 or more on an annualized basis.”

  • On retention, Megji said, “Our overall dollar-based net retention rate was 96%,” and added, “our NRR is a trailing 4-quarter average and therefore, a lagging indicator of more recent trends.”

  • On profitability and timing items, Megji said, “Our gross margin was 88%,” and added that results “benefited from approximately $3 million of operating expenses that shifted from Q1 into the second half.”

  • On earnings and cash generation, Megji reported, “Net income was $24.4 million or $0.10 per share,” and “adjusted free cash flow was $34.4 million in the quarter or 17% of revenue.”

  • On capital allocation and liquidity, Megji said, “we bought back $45 million…at an average price of $6.11 per share,” and ended Q1 with “approximately $424.6 million” in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities.

  • On StackAI deal terms and margin impact, Megji said the transaction includes “approximately $75 million in upfront cash consideration,” and “StackAI is expected to represent approximately a 1 percentage point drag on operating margins in Q2 and the second half of FY ’27.”

Q&A

  • Robert Oliver, Baird: asked why buy StackAI now and integration speed; CEO Rogers: said customers wanted automation “extend those workflows to third-party systems,” and added, “why not deliver it to our customers today?”

  • Oliver, Baird: asked about verticalized go-to-market and whether StackAI requires FTEs; CEO Rogers: tied GTM work to “multiproduct…multi-buying center,” plus “customer health” and “sales productivity,” and said focus is “better serving our ICP.”

  • Matthew Bullock, BofA: asked what drove tech vertical returning to growth and whether Anthropic contributed; CFO Megji: said it was “primarily expansion,” driven by “AI Studio…now…AI Teammates,” plus “seat expansion,” while noting, “We haven’t factored that into our guidance.”

  • Bullock, BofA: asked about $100,000+ customer count softness; CFO Megji: said it was “sequentially flat,” cited quarter-length effects, and added, “we tend to view that metric on a year-over-year basis.”

  • Patrick Walravens, Citizens JMP: asked how Asana breaks through agent “clutter”; CEO Rogers: emphasized differentiation in “Work Graph,” “multiplayer mode,” “shared memory,” and governance, and said messaging starts with “Work Innovation Summit on June 4.”

  • Jackson Ader, KeyBanc: asked if $100,000 AI Studio metric is AI Studio spend; CFO Megji: “they’re $100,000 on the SKU, AI Studio,” and added AI Studio spend is “a double-digit percentage” of core spend.

  • Ader, KeyBanc: asked StackAI buyer persona; CEO Rogers: pointed to “AI centers of excellence,” saying the buying center can be in “operations teams” or “IT teams.”

  • Steven Enders, Citi: asked how Studio adoption changes seat behavior and Teammates feedback; CFO Megji: said Studio adopters are “the strongest NRR cohort,” and for Teammates, “the pipe is growing fairly rapidly.”

  • Josh Baer, Morgan Stanley: asked what must happen to return to 100% NRR and reaccelerate; CEO Rogers: said levers include “multiproduct platform,” “customer health,” “sales productivity,” and “operating velocity.”

  • William Fitzsimmons, Piper Sandler: asked what’s driving NRR improvement; CFO Megji: said Q1 in-quarter NRR improvement to 97% was “coming both on GRR and expansion,” with “the larger piece…on expansion,” and noted “Next quarter, we have the large customer downgrade kind of laps off.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on competitive noise in AI agents, durability of tech-vertical recovery, and whether $100,000+ customer adds were stalling, including, “it’s just so noisy right now,” from Patrick Walravens (Citizens JMP).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive-cautious in guidance framing, repeatedly stressing prudence, including, “We haven’t factored that into our guidance,” and, “we need some additional positive inflection to get more constructive,” from CFO Megji.

  • Compared with the previous quarter, management maintained similar caution on PLG headwinds and tech stabilization assumptions, while the current call’s tone leaned more confident around AI monetization and added a major strategic action via StackAI.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • A key structural change was finance leadership: Q4 FY 2026 featured outgoing CFO Sonalee Parekh and announced Aziz Megji as incoming CFO; Q1 FY 2027 was Megji’s first call as CFO.

  • Q1 FY 2027 introduced a new strategy catalyst not present in Q4: Rogers announced “the acquisition of StackAI,” positioning it as cross-system orchestration that “accelerates our road map by over a year.”

  • Guidance moved up in phrasing and figures: FY 2027 revenue guidance increased to “$855.5 million to $863.5 million” (Q1) from “$850 million to $858 million” (Q4), and operating margin guidance rose to “at least 9.75%” from “at least 9.5%.”

  • Both quarters emphasized PLG as a headwind and tech stabilization as not embedded in outlook; Q1 reiterated, “guidance does not factor in the improvement…in the tech vertical,” consistent with Q4’s “too early to call a bottom” positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Megji said PLG remains a headwind, stating, “our guidance continues to assume approximately a 2-point drag on ARR growth from PLG,” tied to “AI search and LLM-driven experiences.”

  • StackAI integration and profitability risk was acknowledged via margin impact: “approximately a 1 percentage point drag on operating margins in Q2 and the second half of FY ’27,” according to Megji.

  • Dilution remained an explicit concern: “We recognize that stock-based compensation and dilution remain elevated,” Megji said, adding SBC assumptions “remain in the low 20s as a percentage of revenue for the fiscal year.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a combination of steady single-digit revenue growth, expanding profitability, and improving in-quarter retention dynamics, while making a major strategic move to acquire StackAI to extend AI workflow orchestration across enterprise systems; guidance kept PLG as a headwind and excluded recent tech-vertical improvement, while emphasizing AI products’ growing contribution and a planned deeper AI outlook update on the Q2 call.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: GOOG, NFLX, CRSR, PG
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) and Procter & Gamble (PG) have attracted bullish upgrades, with analysts pointing to attractive valuations following periods of underperformance and untapped growth potential. Meanwhile, Corsair Gaming (CRSR) and Alphabet (GOOG) have been downgraded as analysts express concerns over inflated valuations and competitive pressures in the AI space.

Upgrades

  • Netflix (NFLX): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Julia Ostian. The analyst sees significant total addressable market expansion potential as Netflix pushes into new content categories including live sports, podcasts, and creator content.

    “Even though management says their total addressable market ("TAM") is pretty large, focusing on the main streaming side of the business, I’d say it reaches a ceiling, and it’ll need to work very hard to push the growth higher than the growth of the market itself. To be fair, there are still many countries in the world where a smart TV isn’t present in every house, so the market growth itself should be solid.”

  • The Procter & Gamble Company (PG): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Vladimir Dimitrov, CFA. The analyst believes recent stock underperformance has created an attractive entry point, viewing current market pressures as transitional rather than permanent.

    “The PG stock now looks increasingly attractive, especially if dividend increases from recent years are sustained. Moreover, the stock's earnings multiple now stands at its lowest levels for the past 5-year period, which could result in an additional tailwind for anyone buying at current levels and remaining patient.”

Downgrades

  • Corsair Gaming (CRSR): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Wolf Report. The analyst views the stock’s rapid surge driven by AI-related news as an overreaction not justified by fundamentals, with the B2B pivot introducing additional risks.

    “While the company's fundamental upsides remain, and I do believe that Corsair is a compelling buy at a low price, a cheap price is not what we have here. I believe that thanks to an AI-heavy market, the overreaction induced by two pieces of news, and expectations as well as earnings has delivered a return that I believe will quickly normalize again.”

  • Alphabet (GOOG): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Michael B Howard. The analyst exited the position believing the current valuation overestimates AI dominance while underestimating execution risks across cloud and autonomous vehicle segments.

    “Google's work ethos is still lagging, which long (supposedly) justified the market's lower valuation of the company. We have seen recent evidence of Google's lagging work ethos: being ready with AI but still late to the game to release Gemini, which caused the company to lose significant market share. I believe that ethos remains, with Google's nonchalance about selling TPUs when they have been in production for internal use for ten years.”

data.title: ByteDance working on AI chip similar to Groq offerings: report
data.description:
• Chinese technology giant ByteDance (BDNCE) is working on developing artificial intelligence processors that are similar to offerings from Nvidia (NVDA) partner Groq, The Information reported.
  • The chip, which is still in the early stages, would be able to run AI models at a low cost, the news outlet added, citing three people with knowledge of the project. It is working with China-based InnoStar Semiconductor to integrate its memory technology into the new chips, the news outlet added.
  • In addition, ByteDance is also working on a different chip for video algorithms, and other media reports have suggested ByteDance is working on a CPU chip for its inferencing needs.
  • ByteDance did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Nvidia licensed the majority of Groq's intellectual property and hired many of its employees in December 2025 in a $20B deal. In March, Nvidia unveiled a language processing unit based off Groq's technology at its annual GTC event.

data.title: SA Asks: Micron's stock has soared in May; is it now overvalued?
data.description:
Micron shares have soared nearly 80% over the past 30 days amid surging demand for memory products thanks to the proliferation of AI.

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Ricardo Fernandez and Dr. Christopher Davis of Quad 7 Capital  if they thought the stock was now overvalued.

Ricardo Fernandez: Since I first laid eyes on Micron (MU), I was perplexed why it was so cheap, not only relative to growth or semiconductor peers but also on an absolute basis. Last August 2025, after posting good results, the stock was at 6x PE at $150, then post 4Q25 results, which I call its Nvidia (NVDA) moment, Micron's 2026 earnings guidance was 5x 2025, and the stock was cheap again at 6x PE. 

Many SA and Wall Street analysts argued that the memory chip sector was highly cyclical and a bust was around the corner. I wholeheartedly disagreed and just a few weeks ago wrote that MU should garner at least a 15x PE, and it would still be cheap vs. peers and the S&P 500 (SPX), and that its FCF yield and ROIC required a significant re-rate. A subsequent report on Sandisk (SNDK) and NVDA results further confirmed that the memory chip sector is breaking out of its low-value commodity business.

Dr. Christopher Davis of Quad 7 Capital: No, not yet. AI is now becoming industrialized. Devices were certainly cyclical, as a set number of units would be produced in the past, so there was sort of a known limit to demand. With AI, we are now based on usage and inference. Consumption. Certainly, there will be some cyclicality to it down the road, but right now demand is insatiable. 

Is it overvalued? Based on expected earnings growth over the next year, it is still not overvalued, and we had predicted the stock could be re-rated higher. The question is whether this growth continues. As we branch further into humanoid tech and autonomous vehicles, and AI is adopted more broadly, there remains a runway of growth. But comps are going to be very tough to lap in fiscal 2028 without ongoing heavy investment. 

When will the stock face problems? If and when you see companies pull back on AI capex spending. That is when the Street will see steam let out. But it is hard not to think this is like 1999, with parabolic moves in stocks related to new technology. The Knicks are in the NBA finals. There are a lot of parallels to the dot-com mania. Yet, from a pure earnings perspective, MU's share price is rather justified. Perhaps a little quick, which could lead to some big drawdowns on red days or bad market days. 

However, we are still several quarters away from the growth rate being at risk of normalizing. Micron’s multiple, while having expanded slightly, sits well below peers as earnings estimates have more than doubled in the past six weeks.

Quad 7 is the leader of SA investing group Bad Beat Investing. 

  • Top Semiconductor Stocks
  • Trending AI Stocks

data.title: South Bow CEO cites need for 'durable' permit before Keystone XL pipeline restart
data.description:
South Bow (SOBO) wants proof that ‌a U.S. presidential permit is "durable" before it agrees to proceed with a partial revival of the Keystone XL oil pipeline, CEO Bevin Wirzba said Thursday.

The company is keenly ​aware of "sovereign risk," or the possibility that a government unilaterally moves to ​block or halt the construction of the proposed 550K bbl/day Prairie Connector pipeline, Wirzba told the Energy Roundtable conference in Calgary.

"We lost, historically, a presidential permit across the border," the CEO said, referring to the previous ​Keystone XL project, which was canceled by former President Biden in 2021. "So we still need to solve that sovereign risk, and we're working on that within the United States and Canada."

President Trump signed an order in April that granted a cross-border permit to Prairie Connector.

A proposal in the U.S. Congress that would explicitly prohibit a president from revoking cross-border pipeline permits without congressional approval is "a big step forward," Wirzba said, but it does not provide "complete durability" of the key permits, making it difficult to allocate capitalto pipeline projects that straddle the U.S. and Canada.

"We may be able to get to commercialization, but it’s solving the sovereign risks that we need to navigate," the CEO said.

South Bow (SOBO) said Friday it has secured 20-year binding commitments for firm transportation service on Prairie Connector and will continue advancing the proposed project toward a final investment decision targeted for mid-2027.

If sanctioned, the 380-km Prairie Connector would extend from Hardisty, Alberta, to the Canada-U.S. border, where it would connect to Bridger Pipeline's downstream facilities.

data.title: Genesco outlines $40M-$50M cost program through fiscal ’29 while raising full-year EPS guidance to $2-$2.40
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Genesco (GCO) Q1 fiscal 2027

Management View

  • “I’m pleased to report after a strong finish to fiscal ’26 that we are off to a very good start to the year, delivering our seventh consecutive quarter of positive comparable sales and first quarter results that exceeded our expectations across the board.” (Chairman, President, CEO & Interim CFO Mimi Vaughn)

  • “For the quarter, comps were fueled by mid-single-digit increases at Journeys and high single-digit increases at Johnston & Murphy, offset to some extent by declines at Schuh as we began the pullback from promotional activity.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “Journeys added to its run of comp gains, up 5% on top of an 8% increase last year.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “We opened 21 new 4.0 stores in the quarter and now have 105 completed to date.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “Our 4.0 store rollout continues with this new crop of stores also delivering in excess of a 25% sales lift.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “At Schuh, we are putting the building blocks in place to support profitable growth.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “Comps were down 9% in Q1, which was in part intentional as we prioritized and achieved more full price selling and more controlled markdowns.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “We’re seeing encouraging green shoots at Johnston & Murphy as the brand delivered a strong quarter with a 7% comp gain.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “We’ve now completed the wind down of the Levi’s license and are excited for the fall launch of our newest license, Wrangler Footwear, which positions us for healthy growth going forward.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “We’re expecting IEEPA refunds of approximately $23 million to $25 million, which we have already filed for but are not included on our financials this quarter nor in our outlook.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “With this, we are announcing a new $40 million to $50 million cost program between now and fiscal ’29 aimed at structurally reducing our cost base beyond our ongoing efforts.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “Revenue for the quarter increased 3% to $487 million, driven by overall comparable sales growth of 2%.” (Senior Director of FP&A and IR Darryl MacQuarrie) “Adjusted gross margin for the quarter was 47%, up 30 basis points versus last year.” (MacQuarrie) “Adjusted SG&A expense was 51.9% of sales, leveraging 60 basis points versus last year.” (MacQuarrie)

Outlook

  • “Our upwardly revised full year guidance now assumes… adjusted operating income of approximately $34 million to $40 million compared to prior expectations of $32 million to $38 million.” (MacQuarrie) “We are raising our full year earnings per share guidance range to $2 to $2.40.” (MacQuarrie)

  • “For the second quarter specifically… we now expect more top line pressure and a little less gross margin pickup at Schuh, leading to flat to slightly down overall comps.” (MacQuarrie) “Total sales down 3% to 4% versus last year.” (MacQuarrie) “Gross margin to increase 50 to 70 basis points.” (MacQuarrie)

  • “We expect Q2 to be the most pressured quarter year-over-year with improvement thereafter driven by higher sales volumes and continued gross margin recapture.” (MacQuarrie) “We expect second quarter operating loss to be in line with to slightly worse than last year, with earnings per share expected to be approximately $0.20 to $0.30 lower primarily due to the lower tax benefit.” (MacQuarrie)

Financial Results

  • “Adjusted operating loss improved by $4 million to $23.9 million compared to a loss of $27.9 million last year.” (MacQuarrie) “Adjusted diluted loss per share was $2.18 compared to a loss of $2.05 last year.” (MacQuarrie)

  • “Inventory at quarter end was up 6% versus last year, driven by Journeys, reflecting investments in our new brand development, support for 4.0 store expansion and increased inventory in key growth product categories.” (MacQuarrie) “Capital expenditures during the quarter totaled $15 million and were focused primarily on Journeys 4.0 remodels.” (MacQuarrie)

  • “We ended the quarter with 1,208 total stores following 2 openings and 30 closures.” (MacQuarrie) “We did not repurchase shares during the quarter and have $29.8 million remaining under our existing authorization.” (MacQuarrie)

Q&A

  • Joseph Civello, Truist Securities, Inc.: “Can you provide a little more color… and then your outlook for each category this year…?” CEO Vaughn: “The sandal business has been just on fire this spring… Low profile is gaining a lot of traction… There’s no one thing that I would call out, but… the diversity of different opportunities across the assortment, across the brand mix.”

  • Civello, Truist Securities, Inc.: “Is there any way to parse out… the acceleration… at Johnston & Murphy?” CEO Vaughn: “New customer growth is up double digits… particularly with a younger customer… we are excited to announce that we’ve extended Peyton into a new fall campaign.”

  • Civello, Truist Securities, Inc.: “Just on the $40 million to $50 million cost program… where you see the lowest hanging fruit?” CEO Vaughn: “We’re looking at areas like selling salaries… robotics and automation within our distribution centers… some marketing spend optimization work as well.”

  • Mantero Moreno-Cheek, Jefferies: “Should we expect [Schuh]… to inflect positive as early as 1Q next year?” CEO Vaughn: “We do expect a nice pickup in gross margin through the course of the year… A lot depends on the consumer environment.”

  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: “When you get… the refund, how do you anticipate accounting for it?” CEO Vaughn: “We are using the gain contingency method… that means that you actually book the dollars when you receive them.”

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research Partners: “Why the sales guide for the year hasn’t changed?” CEO Vaughn: “We are less bullish on the U.K. environment… really specifically for the second quarter… it’s a very dynamic situation in the U.K. right now.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly positive: “Great quarter. Congratulations.” (Joseph Civello, Truist Securities, Inc.) and “Congrats on the quarter.” (Mantero Moreno-Cheek, Jefferies) were followed by detailed follow-ups on Schuh timing, sales-guide consistency, and cost-savings cadence.

  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks, more cautious in Q&A on the U.K.: “We remain confident in continued momentum in North America” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) contrasted with “We are less bullish on the U.K. environment… really specifically for the second quarter.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from broad momentum to more specific structural actions: Q4 highlighted “clear momentum as we head into fiscal ’27” (Chairman, President & CEO Vaughn) while Q1 added “a new $40 million to $50 million cost program between now and fiscal ’29.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership/finance structure changed from a CFO-led financial section in Q4 to an interim setup in Q1: “I will assume the role of Interim CFO” (Chairman, President & CEO Vaughn, Q4) and Q1 was led by “Chairman, President, CEO & Interim CFO” Vaughn with financial detail delivered by MacQuarrie.

  • Guidance direction improved quarter-over-quarter: Q4 provided “adjusted EPS… in the range of $1.90 to $2.30” (Consultant & Principal Accounting Officer Cassandra Harris) while Q1 updated to “an increased full year EPS guidance range of $2 to $2.40.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • The core strategy remained consistent around Journeys transformation and Schuh promotion reset, but Q1 added incremental cost actions and tariff refund specificity: “we are announcing a new $40 million to $50 million cost program” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) and “expecting IEEPA refunds of approximately $23 million to $25 million.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

Risks and Concerns

  • “In Q2 so far… a give up at Schuh, where the economy and geopolitical pressures are taking a toll.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “We are really affected right now because of the proximity to the Iran conflict and consumer sentiment has been affected by that.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

  • “Quarterly tax rate volatility due to the valuation allowance… will distort quarterly earnings per share comparisons.” (MacQuarrie) “We recommend investors focus on operating income trends as the cleanest read on underlying performance.” (MacQuarrie)

  • “Tariff headwinds were highest in Q1 due to inventory flow timing.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn) “Our mitigating actions around pricing and sourcing diversification have helped ease these headwinds.” (CEO & Interim CFO Vaughn)

Final Takeaway

Genesco’s management emphasized a Q1 start “that exceeded our expectations across the board” (Chairman, President, CEO & Interim CFO Mimi Vaughn), raised full-year EPS guidance to “$2 to $2.40” (Vaughn), and outlined a new “$40 million to $50 million cost program between now and fiscal ’29” (Vaughn). Prepared remarks and Q&A repeatedly framed Journeys’ ongoing momentum (including a “25% sales lift” from 4.0 stores) and Johnston & Murphy’s “7% comp gain,” while positioning Schuh’s promo pullback as intentionally pressuring near-term comps amid a more cautious U.K. backdrop and geopolitical-related demand uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: White House proposes stiffer regional requirements under USMCA -- WSJ
data.description:
American auto manufacturers may be forced to increase the percentage of vehicles that are domestically assembled if the White House is successful in enforcing new rules within the trade agreement between the U.S., Mexico, and Canada. 

Under the terms of the current U.S., Mexico, Canada Agreement ((USMCA)) – enacted during the first Trump Administration—U.S. vehicles are exempt from most import tariffs if at least 75% of the components are sourced from North American suppliers. The Trump Administration now wants to make 50% of those components sourced from American suppliers, as well as bump up the 75% threshold to 82%, according to sources cited by The Wall Street Journal.

Non-compliant vehicles currently face a 25% import tariff.

Automakers will likely push back on any alterations to USMCA, arguing that increasing the threshold of domestic sourcing will lead to production delays and higher auto prices for consumers, along with making affordably priced vehicles too expensive to make in the U.S.  

General Motors (GM) CEO Mary Barra cautioned that stiffer regional content requirements will harm U.S. competitiveness and inconsistency in U.S. trade policies makes capital investments challenging.

“I need clarity, and then I need consistency,” Barra said, noting that automotive supply chains can’t be redesigned overnight.

Automakers fear an accelerated rollout of changes to USMCA thresholds. Manufacturers typically allocate five to six years to redesign production lines and adjust supply chains.

“Everything can be moved over time, but we also have to do it effectively, and we have to do it in a manner where we’re still competitive globally,” Barra added.

U.S./Mexico trade talks are currently underway in Mexico City and expected to continue until July.

Shares of GM (GM) and Stellantis (STLA) retreated on the news, while Ford (F) remains propped up by its energy storage ambitions.

data.title: Allied Gold drops after report $4B Zijin deal faces opposition in China
data.description:
Allied Gold (AAUC) (AAUC:CA) 5.6% after a report that the company's $4 billion sale to China's Zijin Mining (ZIJMY) is facing delays due to opposition from regulators in Beijing. The deal spread on Friday widened to $7, the most since the $44 a share deal was announced in January.

China's regulator, the National Development and Reform Commission, has questioned the premium Zijin is paying as well as the risks associated with Allied’s gold mine in Mali, according to a  Financial Times report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

A delay in China's regulatory approval has become a big issue, with some questioning if the deal will close as planned, according to the FT report. The original agreement had a deadline of Friday, which can be extended if both sides agree.

 “There is strong industrial and commercial logic for this transaction, and both parties continue to work diligently towards closing," an Allied spokesperson told the FT.

Late last month, Allied Gold (AAUC) confirmed the normal course conduct of business at its operating mines in Mali amid reports of conflict in the country.

Zijin Mining (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY) agreed to buy Allied Gold (AAUC) for ~C$5.5B (US$4B) in cash in January.

data.title: OpenAI unveils Rosalind Biodefense program for biodefense, pandemic preparedness
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) said it is launching a program called Rosalind Biodefense to help trusted developers build new biodefense and pandemic preparedness capabilities.

The AI start-up backed by Microsoft (MSFT) is also expanding trusted access to its AI model GPT‑Rosalind for select U.S. government and allied partners supporting public health and biodefense missions. Last month, OpenAI launched the AI model GPT‑Rosalind, which is designed to accelerate drug discovery and support biology-related research work.

"Advances in biology can strengthen our ability to prevent, detect, and respond to biological threats. Our goal is to help build a more robust ecosystem – giving trusted defenders frontier AI to develop and operate new defenses for public health and biodefense," said OpenAI in a post on X.

The company said in a blog post that the strategy includes equipping defenders through trusted access to advanced AI tools, accelerating the development of medical countermeasures, building earlier warning systems, strengthening diagnostics, preparedness, and response capabilities, and supporting an evaluation ecosystem.

OpenAI noted that it will sponsor access to GPT‑Rosalind and provide launch support to trusted developers building frontier biosecurity applications that can strengthen societal defenses and build pandemic preparedness. This includes work across areas like epidemiological modeling, early detection, screening, preparedness, non-pharmaceutical interventions, or NPIs, and other public-health-relevant capabilities.

The company added that it is collaborating with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or LLNL, which is applying AI to advance biopreparedness and bioresilience.

OpenAI is also working with Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory, which intends to integrate GPT‑Rosalind into a protein‑engineering platform to accelerate screening of mutant enzymes for therapeutics, countermeasure development, and emerging biothreat characterization.

In addition, OpenAI is extending access to GPT‑Rosalind to the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations, or CEPI, which is focused on its 100 Days Mission to accelerate the development of vaccines against epidemic and pandemic threats, including the current Ebola outbreak.

The AI giant noted that the Rosalind Biodefense Program is open to qualified applicants globally, and the company is looking to support more organizations building defensive applications that can use GPT‑Rosalind to improve societal resilience.
